claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**What is the age of your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex, who's 13, and Blake, who's 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you check eligibility for both Alex (age 13) and Blake (age 15).

Since both of your children are under 16, let me work through the eligibility criteria for each of them. Let me check the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to check a few conditions for children under 16:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is an important requirement - children aren't eligible if they're working 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 13, and Blake doesn't have a job either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's good to know. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I need to confirm that neither child is receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits in their own name

For most 13 and 15-year-olds, this wouldn't apply, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently in an apprenticeship?**

This is important because certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility:
- English apprenticeships (all types) would disqualify them
- Scottish Modern Apprenticeships would disqualify them  
- Welsh apprenticeships (except Foundation level) would disqualify them
- Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales are approved and wouldn't disqualify them

Given their ages (13 and 15), it's likely neither is in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in any apprenticeships. They're both still at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! They're both still in school, which is great. Let me move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status as the claimant.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through ANY of these paths:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - This is the most common route
2. **Have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Have pre-settled status** and meet one of these conditions:
   - Working and earning (or expect to earn) above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months
   - Actively looking for work (up to 91 days)
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself and family (and not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
   - Studying with sufficient resources
   - Are a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. **Living abroad as a Crown servant** or in certain qualifying countries

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That meets the residency requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake?**

You can meet this requirement through ANY of these ways:

1. **Living with the children** - You live in the same household as them
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care, including food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. **Paying maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit payment

Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do you support them in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live with Alex here at home, but Blake doesn't live with me. I do help out with Blake though - I contribute towards their upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So you live with Alex, and you contribute towards Blake's upkeep even though Blake doesn't live with you.

**For Blake specifically - can you tell me a bit more about your contributions?**

To meet the responsibility requirement, your contributions need to equal at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be. This can include:
- Money for food, clothes, or pocket money
- Housing costs
- Presents
- Other financial support

These can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments.

Do your contributions for Blake meet or exceed the Child Benefit amount? (For context, Child Benefit is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute money regularly for Blake - I help with clothes and school dinners and things like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - it sounds like you're meeting the responsibility requirement for both children. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

This is important because:
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time
- If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the decision

Are you the only person claiming (or intending to claim) for these children, or is someone else already claiming for either of them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No one else is claiming for Alex. But for Blake - well, Blake doesn't live with me, they live with their other parent, so I'm not sure about that situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about that. This is an important situation to clarify.

**Is Blake's other parent (who Blake lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

If they are, there are a few possibilities:
- If they're already claiming and you both agree that they should continue claiming, then they would be the claimant (not you)
- If they're claiming but you both agree you should claim instead, the claim can be transferred to you
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

Also, it's worth noting that generally, the person the child lives with has priority for claiming Child Benefit. Since Blake lives with their other parent, they would typically be the one to claim.

Can you tell me:
1. Is Blake's other parent currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
2. If so, have you discussed who should be the claimant?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake's other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for them. We've talked about it and agreed that I should be the one to claim since I'm contributing to Blake's upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's very helpful to know - thank you. Since Blake's other parent isn't claiming and you've both agreed that you should claim, that satisfies the requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about the children's living situations.

**For both Alex and Blake - are either of them currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally (at home)?

This affects eligibility because there are special time-limited rules for children in hospital or care.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives at home with me normally. Blake has been in local authority care for a bit, but comes home to stay with me on weekends.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Blake is in local authority care (even though they come home on weekends), I need to check the duration rules for children in care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question about Blake's situation.

**For Blake, who is in local authority care:**

Child Benefit can continue in certain circumstances:
- For up to 8 weeks when a child first goes into care, OR
- If the child spends **24 hours or more per week at home** with you

You mentioned Blake comes home to stay with you on weekends. 

**Can you tell me:**
1. How long has Blake been in care?
2. When Blake comes home on weekends, is it for 24 hours or more per week?

For example, if Blake stays from Friday evening through Sunday, that would likely be more than 24 hours.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake's been in care for about a month now. And yes, when they come home on weekends, it's definitely more than 24 hours - they come Friday after school and stay until Sunday evening.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's very helpful information. Since Blake has been in care for about a month (which is less than 8 weeks) AND spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you, Blake still qualifies under the care duration rules. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I have one final important question:

**Is the local council paying towards the accommodation or maintenance costs for either Alex or Blake?**

This is particularly relevant for Blake since they're in local authority care. If the council is paying for Blake's care and accommodation, this would affect eligibility for Child Benefit for Blake.

For Alex, who lives with you normally, this typically wouldn't apply unless the council is providing some specific support.

Can you confirm:
- Is the council paying for Blake's care placement?
- Is the council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: The council is paying for Blake's care placement, yes. For Alex, no - the council isn't paying anything for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarity. This is an important distinction that affects the eligibility for each child differently.

Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for both Alex and Blake:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here's what I found:

---

### **For Alex (age 13): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

You **can claim Child Benefit** for Alex. Alex meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working full-time
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- Lives with you at home
- No council payments for their accommodation or maintenance

---

### **For Blake (age 15): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you **cannot claim Child Benefit** for Blake at this time. While Blake meets most requirements and you're clearly supporting them, there's one critical issue:

**The local council is paying for Blake's care placement.** Under Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim when the local council is paying towards a child's accommodation or maintenance, even though:
- Blake spends weekends at home with you (more than 24 hours per week)
- Blake has only been in care for about a month
- You contribute to Blake's upkeep

---

### **What You Should Do Next:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit for Alex** - You can do this online at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

2. **Monitor Blake's situation** - If circumstances change and the council stops paying for Blake's care placement, you may become eligible to claim for Blake at that point.

3. **If you have questions about Blake's situation**, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for advice specific to your circumstances.

I know this is a mixed result, but you should definitely claim for Alex. Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify?

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives with you at home. You meet the UK residency requirement (living in Manchester), you have responsibility for Alex (living together), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement and have responsibility for Blake through regular financial contributions towards their upkeep (clothes, school dinners, etc.). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Although Blake is in local authority care, they spend more than 24 hours per week at home with you (Friday after school to Sunday evening) and have been in care for only about a month (under 8 weeks), which would normally allow continued eligibility. However, the local council is paying for Blake's care placement. Under Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim Child Benefit for a child when the local council is paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives at home and meets all qualifying criteria. However, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because the local council is paying for Blake's care placement, even though Blake spends weekends at home and the claimant contributes to Blake's upkeep.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives with you at home. You meet the UK residency requirement (living in Manchester), you have responsibility for Alex (living together), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement and have responsibility for Blake through regular financial contributions towards their upkeep (clothes, school dinners, etc.). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Although Blake is in local authority care, they spend more than 24 hours per week at home with you (Friday after school to Sunday evening) and have been in care for only about a month (under 8 weeks), which would normally allow continued eligibility. However, the local council is paying for Blake's care placement. Under Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim Child Benefit for a child when the local council is paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives at home and meets all qualifying criteria. However, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because the local council is paying for Blake's care placement, even though Blake spends weekends at home and the claimant contributes to Blake's upkeep.
